title: English Podcast | Travel & Vacations
youtubeTitle: The English You Need Before Your Next Trip
youtubeDescription:
Lost luggage, jet lag, and a hotel booked for the wrong month — Emma and Mike share their funniest travel stories in clear, natural English for B1-B2 learners. Follow the on-screen transcript to build real travel vocabulary: flights, airports, hotels, street food, and trip planning. Perfect listening practice if you want to sound natural in everyday American and Australian conversations.

🎧 Turn on captions (CC), relax, and just listen.
👉 Subscribe for a new easy English conversation every week!

#LearnEnglish #AmericanEnglish #EnglishPodcast #TravelEnglish #B1English

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Hey everyone, welcome back! Today we're talking about something we all love — travel.
turns.enTts: [warm] Hey everyone, welcome back! Today we're talking about something we all love — travel.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Oh, this is going to be a fun one. I've been thinking about my next trip all week.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Perfect timing then. So Mike, if you could go anywhere in the world, where would you go?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Honestly? New Zealand. I've dreamed about it for years — the mountains, the lakes, everything.
turns.enTts: Honestly? [excited] New Zealand. I've dreamed about it for years — the mountains, the lakes, everything.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Oh really? I didn't know you were such a nature person.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Big time. Give me a hiking trail and a tent, and I'm happy. What about you?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: For me, it's Japan. I want to see Tokyo, but also the small towns and the old temples.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That sounds amazing. So you're more of a city traveler, then?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Mostly, yeah. I love walking around a new city with no plan, just getting a little lost.
turns.enTts: Mostly, yeah. [laughs] I love walking around a new city with no plan, just getting a little lost.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: See, that would stress me out. Cities are so noisy and crowded.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: But that's the fun part! The energy, the street food, the little shops everywhere.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Fair enough. And where do beach holidays fit in for you?
turns.enTts: [curious] Fair enough. And where do beach holidays fit in for you?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Hmm, I get bored at the beach after two days, to be honest. I need something to do.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Not me. Lying in the sun with a good book? That's my idea of a perfect afternoon.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Okay, so let's talk about planning. When you book a trip, how far ahead do you start?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Usually about three months before. Flights are much cheaper if you book early.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Three months? Wow, you're organized. I once booked a flight two days before leaving.
turns.enTts: Three months? [surprised] Wow, you're organized. [laughs] I once booked a flight two days before leaving.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Two days? That must have cost you a fortune!

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: It did, actually. I've learned my lesson — now I at least check prices a few weeks ahead.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Timing matters too. I always try to travel in the low season, when places are quieter.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That makes sense. Fewer tourists, better prices. But the weather can be a risk, right?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: True. I once went to Scotland in November. It rained every single day.
turns.enTts: True. [sighs] I once went to Scotland in November. It rained every single day.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Oh no. Okay, next question — are you an itinerary person or a go-with-the-flow person?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Full itinerary. I plan every day before I leave — museums, restaurants, everything.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Of course you do. I just pick one or two must-see places and leave the rest open.
turns.enTts: [laughs] Of course you do. I just pick one or two must-see places and leave the rest open.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Doesn't that get expensive, though? If you don't plan, you can't really control your budget.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I set a daily limit instead. Say, fifty dollars a day for food and fun, and I stick to it.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That's actually smart. Maybe I should try being spontaneous for once.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Well, you'll get your chance soon — you're flying to Portugal next month, aren't you?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: I am, and don't get me started on that flight. It leaves at six in the morning.
turns.enTts: I am, and [sighs] don't get me started on that flight. It leaves at six in the morning.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Speaking of flying, are you one of those people who gets to the airport super early?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Always. I like to be there three hours before an international flight.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Three hours? I usually arrive late and run to the gate.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That sounds so stressful! What if the security line is really long?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: It happened once. I nearly missed a flight to Rome because of my belt and my laptop.
turns.enTts: [laughs] It happened once. I nearly missed a flight to Rome because of my belt and my laptop.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: See? That's why I check in online the night before. It saves so much stress.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: True. Do you usually check a bag, or do you travel with carry-on only?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Carry-on only, if I can. I hate standing at the baggage belt after a long flight.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Same here. Besides, airlines lose bags more often than people think.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Oh, tell me about it. My suitcase got lost on the way to Lisbon once.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: No way! How long did it take to get it back?
turns.enTts: [surprised] No way! How long did it take to get it back?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Four days. I had to buy clothes and a toothbrush, and the airline paid me back later.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That's exactly why I always keep a change of clothes in my hand luggage.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Smart. Okay, important question. Window seat or aisle seat?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Window, definitely. I love watching the clouds, and I can lean against the wall to sleep.
turns.enTts: [excited] Window, definitely. I love watching the clouds, and I can lean against the wall to sleep.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: I'm team aisle. I drink so much water that I have to get up every hour.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Fair enough. So what's the longest flight you've ever taken?
turns.enTts: [laughs] Fair enough. So what's the longest flight you've ever taken?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Fourteen hours, from London to Singapore. My legs felt like wood at the end.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: How do you survive flights like that? I can never sleep on planes.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Noise-cancelling headphones, an eye mask, and no coffee after take-off.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Do those neck pillows actually help? Mine always ends up on the floor.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Honestly, mine too. My real trick is to set my watch to the time at my destination.
turns.enTts: [chuckles] Honestly, mine too. My real trick is to set my watch to the time at my destination.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Oh, that helps with jet lag too, right?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Exactly. Get some sunlight when you land, and stay awake until the local bedtime.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I should try that. After my last trip, I was waking up at four in the morning for a week.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Jet lag is honestly the worst part of flying east.
turns.enTts: [sighs] Jet lag is honestly the worst part of flying east.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Agreed. But arriving is the best feeling, dropping your bags and falling onto a hotel bed.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: If the hotel is nice, that is. I've had some real surprises with hotels.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Ooh, that sounds like a story. Good surprises or bad ones?
turns.enTts: [curious] Ooh, that sounds like a story. Good surprises or bad ones?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: A bit of both. Let's just say the photos on booking websites can be very creative.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Okay, I definitely want to hear those stories. But first — once you land somewhere, are you a hotel person or an Airbnb person?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Honestly, it depends on the trip. For a short city break I book a hotel, but for a week or longer I prefer an apartment with a kitchen.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That makes sense. Cooking a few meals yourself saves so much money, especially in expensive cities.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Exactly. What about you? I remember you used to stay in hostels all the time.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I did, back when I was a student. Hostels are noisy, but you meet people from all over the world in one evening.
turns.enTts: [laughs] I did, back when I was a student. Hostels are noisy, but you meet people from all over the world in one evening.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: True, that social side is hard to beat. But sharing a room with eight strangers? I think I'm too old for that now.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Fair enough. So when you book a place, what do you look at first? The price, the location, the photos?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Location first, always. I'd rather pay a bit more and walk everywhere than save money and spend an hour on the bus every day.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Same here. And I always read the reviews, but only the negative ones. That's where the truth is hiding.
turns.enTts: Same here. And I always read the reviews, but only the negative ones. [whispers] That's where the truth is hiding.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Smart. If five different people mention thin walls or a broken shower, you know it's not bad luck.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Exactly. The photos can lie, but fifty reviews usually don't. Have you ever booked somewhere that looked amazing online and turned out terrible?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Oh, have I. There was this apartment in Rome that looked huge in the photos. When I opened the door, the bed was basically touching the fridge.
turns.enTts: [sighs] Oh, have I. There was this apartment in Rome that looked huge in the photos. When I opened the door, the bed was basically touching the fridge.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: No way! So the wide-angle camera strikes again.
turns.enTts: [laughs] No way! So the wide-angle camera strikes again.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Every single time. Since then, I always check if the reviews mention the size, not just the style.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Good lesson. Actually, that reminds me of my worst booking mistake ever. I once booked a hotel for the wrong month.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Wait, the wrong month? How does that even happen?
turns.enTts: [gasps] Wait, the wrong month? How does that even happen?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I was booking late at night and clicked June instead of July. I only noticed when we arrived at midnight and the receptionist said our reservation was thirty days ago.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Oh no. At midnight, with all your bags? What did you do?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Luckily they had one room left, but it cost twice as much. Now I check my booking dates about five times before I pay.
turns.enTts: [laughs] Luckily they had one room left, but it cost twice as much. Now I check my booking dates about five times before I pay.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Painful, but at least you got a bed. My worst mishap was losing my wallet on my second day in Bangkok.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Oh no, that's my nightmare. Did you get it back?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Never. I had to block my cards from a noodle shop while borrowing the owner's phone. But you know what? People were incredibly kind to me that whole week.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That's actually really sweet. Sometimes the disaster becomes the best story of the trip.
turns.enTts: [warm] That's actually really sweet. Sometimes the disaster becomes the best story of the trip.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Totally. Since then I keep some emergency cash hidden in my luggage and photos of my passport in my email. Just in case.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That's a great tip. Weather is the one thing you can't plan for, though. Rain once ruined my whole beach week in Da Nang.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Ouch. A whole week of rain on a beach holiday sounds pretty depressing.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: It was, at first. But we gave up on the beach and just explored instead — little cafes, markets, museums. It turned into a completely different trip.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: See, that's the real lesson. When the plan falls apart, the trip doesn't end. It just changes direction.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Exactly. And honestly, that rainy week is when I discovered the best food I've ever eaten while traveling.
turns.enTts: Exactly. And honestly, [excited] that rainy week is when I discovered the best food I've ever eaten while traveling.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Okay, now you have to tell me about that. Food is my favorite part of any trip.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: It was this tiny noodle place near the market in Da Nang — I still dream about that bowl. But yes, food really is the best part of any trip. Do you always try the local dishes?
turns.enTts: [excited] It was this tiny noodle place near the market in Da Nang — I still dream about that bowl. But yes, food really is the best part of any trip. Do you always try the local dishes?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Always. For me, tasting local food is half the reason to travel. What about you?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Same here. I never eat at big fast food chains when I'm abroad. It feels like a waste of the trip.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Exactly. Street food is usually my first stop. It's cheap, fresh, and you eat with the locals.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Oh, I love street food markets. The smells, the noise, all the little stalls — it's an experience, not just a meal.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: So tell me, what's the strangest thing you've ever eaten on a trip?
turns.enTts: [curious] So tell me, what's the strangest thing you've ever eaten on a trip?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Probably fried crickets in Thailand. I was scared at first, but honestly, they tasted like salty chips.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: No way! I don't think I could do that. My strangest one was snails in France.
turns.enTts: [laughs] No way! I don't think I could do that. My strangest one was snails in France.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Snails are actually delicious with garlic butter. See? Trying new things pays off.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: True. Food also teaches you a lot about the culture. In Japan, I learned you should never stick chopsticks straight up in your rice.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Right, because it's connected to funerals there. Those small cultural rules are so easy to miss.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: They really are. In some countries, finishing your plate is polite. In others, it means you're still hungry.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That's so confusing! I once kept eating in Italy because the grandmother kept filling my plate.
turns.enTts: [laughs] That's so confusing! I once kept eating in Italy because the grandmother kept filling my plate.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That sounds like a happy problem to have. Do you learn a few words of the language before you go?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Always. Just hello, thank you, and delicious. People smile so warmly when you try their language.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: It opens doors, doesn't it? Okay, next question — do you buy souvenirs, or do you think they're a waste of money?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I used to buy little magnets everywhere, but now I prefer something useful, like local coffee or spices.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: That's smart. I always bring back one small thing from a local market — never from the airport shops.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Airport souvenirs cost twice as much anyway. The best gifts have a story behind them.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Speaking of stories — what's your favorite travel memory of all time?
turns.enTts: [curious] Speaking of stories — what's your favorite travel memory of all time?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: Watching the sunrise over the mountains in Nepal. It was freezing, but nobody said a word. We just watched.
turns.enTts: [warmly] Watching the sunrise over the mountains in Nepal. It was freezing, but nobody said a word. We just watched.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Wow, that gave me goosebumps. Mine is simpler — a tiny family restaurant in Vietnam where the owner taught me to cook noodles.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: See, that's the thing. The best memories are never about famous buildings. They're about people and small moments.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Couldn't agree more. So, before we finish — what's the next trip you're looking forward to?

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: I'm finally going to Portugal this autumn. I've dreamed about Lisbon for years. What about you?
turns.enTts: [excited] I'm finally going to Portugal this autumn. I've dreamed about Lisbon for years. What about you?

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Wait, I'm flying to Portugal next month, remember? You'll have to get all my tips. After that, I'm planning a road trip along the coast of South Korea next spring — just me, a rental car, and a lot of seafood.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: That sounds amazing. You'll have to tell us all about it when you're back.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: Definitely. Well, that's all we have time for today. Thanks so much for listening, everyone.
turns.enTts: [warmly] Definitely. Well, that's all we have time for today. Thanks so much for listening, everyone.

turns.speaker: A
turns.en: We hope this episode made you hungry for your next adventure. Keep practicing your English, and keep exploring.

turns.speaker: B
turns.en: See you in the next episode. Safe travels, everyone. Bye for now!
turns.enTts: [warmly] See you in the next episode. Safe travels, everyone. [chuckles] Bye for now!

fileKeywords: learn english travel and vacations b1 b2 conversation
